title: Tiling around a soil pipe
subtitle: Cut and fit ceramic tiles around a vertical or horizontal waste pipe
excerpt: How to measure, mark, and cut ceramic or porcelain tiles to fit neatly around a soil pipe or waste pipe that passes through a tiled wall or floor, including making internal curved cuts, sealing the joint, and dealing with an off-centre pipe.
sourceNotes: British Ceramic Tile Council tiling guidance. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: An internal curved or notched cut made on a tile using an angle grinder fitted with a diamond blade, a tile drill core bit, or a specialist tile nibbler. Straight cuts can be made with a score-and-snap cutter; curves and holes in tiles require a powered tool or nibbler.

A decorative escutcheon plate that fits around a pipe where it passes through a tiled surface, concealing the gap between the tile cut and the pipe and providing a clean finish. Available in white plastic and chrome for different settings.

content.text: A soil pipe or waste pipe passing through a tiled surface is a common obstacle in bathroom and cloakroom tiling. The challenge is making a clean curved cut in a ceramic or porcelain tile without cracking the tile: score-and-snap cutters cannot make curved cuts. The cleanest approach uses an angle grinder fitted with a diamond dry-cut blade for most cuts, with a core drill bit for full-circle holes, and a
to conceal the joint between the tile edge and the pipe.
Step 1, measure the pipe position accurately

Measure from the pipe centre to the nearest laid tile in both horizontal and vertical directions. Transfer these measurements to the cut tile and mark the pipe centre point with a pencil. Draw a circle at the pipe radius plus 3 mm clearance. The clearance lets the tile slide in without binding and leaves room for the silicone joint between tile and pipe.

If the pipe passes through the centre of a tile, drill a full hole with a diamond core bit at slow speed. Keep the bit wet. If the pipe is at the edge of a tile, use an angle grinder with a diamond blade. Make short straight cuts into the waste area and nibble it away. A
is slower but works on ceramic tiles where a grinder will not fit.

Mark the centre point with masking tape to protect the tile face and prevent the bit skidding. Start the diamond core bit at 45° to create a groove, then bring the drill to 90° and bore at low speed (500 to 800 rpm). Keep the cutting edge cool by dripping water onto the face. Do not force the bit: let it cut. A 40 mm diameter soil pipe needs a 45 to 50 mm core bit; a 32 mm waste pipe needs a 35 to 38 mm bit.
Step 4, fix the tile and seal the joint

Apply tile adhesive and set the tile in position. Once the adhesive has cured, apply a bead of white sanitary-grade silicone sealant into the joint between the tile cut and the pipe. Smooth with a wetted finger. Apply a pipe collar over the pipe to hide the joint: most pipe collars are split so they can be clipped over an existing pipe without disconnecting the pipework.

If the pipe is boxed in and the tile is cut around the boxing, the tile edge meets a flat timber surface. Use flexible decorator's caulk at this joint rather than silicone: caulk is easier to paint over later. For large-format porcelain tiles (over 300 × 300 mm), use only a diamond core drill. A nibbler on thick porcelain cracks the tile from the cut edge.

attrs.symptom: Tile cracks at the edge of the pipe cut during drilling
attrs.cause: Drill speed too fast; too much downward pressure; bit overheating.
attrs.fix: Slow the drill, keep it cool with water, and apply only light pressure. Let the diamond grit do the cutting. On hard porcelain, use a drill rated for porcelain rather than a standard ceramic drill bit.

attrs.symptom: Cut tile does not fit over the pipe — the hole is fractionally too small
attrs.cause: Hole marked at the pipe's nominal diameter rather than its external diameter plus clearance.
attrs.fix: Use a tile file or the edge of a diamond blade to enlarge the hole by small increments, checking the fit frequently.

attrs.symptom: Silicone joint between tile and pipe is discoloured or mouldy within a year
attrs.cause: Standard sanitary-grade silicone used in a poorly ventilated or persistently damp environment; silicone not pressed fully into the joint.
attrs.fix: Cut out the old silicone with a utility knife and re-seal with a mould-resistant silicone rated for bathrooms. Improve ventilation to reduce condensation.
